video_title: I Just Revealed My Current Portfolio…
gist: Anthony Pompliano (interviewed by John Pompliano) reveals his portfolio and macro views: the Mag 7 selloff is inflation-fear + AI-capex-fear driven, but inflation isn't persistent (energy = 60% of the rise) so multiples should re-expand; new Fed chair Warsh pulled an 'economic sleight of hand' (no rate cut but killed forward guidance and changed the inflation metric), with ~60% odds of a 2026 cut. He doesn't believe AI-capex guidance (expects underspend = FCF upside), frames SpaceX and Tesla as AI/compute and physical-AI plays, owns Anduril/Robo Strategy for defense-robotics, and sets a 25-30%/yr Bitcoin base case as institutions compress its volatility.

insights.claim: Pompliano argues the Mag 7 selloff is mostly long-duration assets reacting to inflation fears (Iran war/energy spike) plus AI-capex worries, but he sees inflation as neither high nor persistent — energy drove ~60% of the inflation increase, and with oil below $80 (toward $60) he expects Q3/Q4 inflation below Q2, which would re-expand Mag 7 multiples.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: If inflation rolls over with energy, multiple compression on mega-cap tech reverses — a setup for Mag 7 re-rating higher.
insights.quote: Energy was responsible for 60% of all increase in the inflation number

insights.claim: Pompliano characterizes new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's debut as 'economic sleight of hand': he held rates flat but enacted seismic changes — a task force, a new inflation metric, killing forward guidance, and likely personnel changes — precisely BECAUSE not cutting rates gave him room to do everything else; he grades Warsh a B+.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A regime change at the Fed — no forward guidance and a redefined inflation gauge — raises rate-path uncertainty regardless of the unchanged policy rate.

insights.claim: Pompliano puts ~60% odds on a rate cut before end-2026, calling Warsh 'trigger happy' with his finger over the button awaiting lower inflation, and argues a hike would be premature/ill-advised while flat would be fine.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Positions for a dovish-skewed Fed in 2H26 — fade the rate-hike camp if inflation cools.
insights.quote: I think that before the end of the year, we're likely to get some sort of rate cut

insights.claim: Pompliano separates inflation (an academic rate-of-change concept investors care about) from affordability (what the average American feels) — noting the dollar lost ~30% of purchasing power in 5 years even as the inflation RATE normalizes.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: The affordability/inflation gap is the political pressure point — relevant to fiscal/policy risk even if the CPI prints cooperate.

insights.claim: Pompliano argues rising credit-card delinquencies are a credit-card-rate problem (average ~23% APR), not a sign of consumer weakness — consumer spending is 'off the charts.'
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Discounts a popular bear signal — supports a resilient-consumer base case.

insights.claim: From building his CFO Sylvia product, Pompliano sees the industry shifting from 'token maxing' to efficiency/ROI — yet AI labs' revenue stays high because TAM and adoption grow even as individual customers cut token usage, which he reads as genuine product-market fit (the risk being open-source models eating their lunch).
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Per-customer token efficiency is bullish, not bearish, for frontier-lab revenue — watch open-source as the real competitive threat.

insights.claim: Pompliano doesn't believe the AI capex guidance numbers — he expects actual spend to come in well below projections (e.g., a $10B guide printing ~$6B, 40% less), since firms guide capex like projected revenue.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: If capex underspends guidance, free cash flow surprises to the upside — a contrarian bullish catalyst for Mag 7.
insights.quote: I don't believe the numbers that the guidance is. I actually think that they're going to spend less than that

insights.claim: Pompliano sees persistent AI demand keeping a 'persistent bid' under data centers, power and chips (citing a firm shifting work to 1am-10am for cheaper, less-congested model usage, and orbital data centers) — overbuild is possible eventually but not near-term.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Infrastructure (compute/power) demand stays tight near-term — supports the picks-and-shovels layer over fear of an imminent capex bust.

insights.claim: Pompliano flags Google as now cheaper than Apple yet growing faster (sold off on AI-capex fears), and notes Mag 7 valuations haven't moved in six months while the other 493 S&P names ran — letting investors buy the same businesses at six-month-old prices after more growth.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Mega-cap tech is relatively attractive after lagging — a rotation-back-into-Mag-7 setup.
insights.quote: Google is now cheaper than Apple, but it's growing faster than Apple

insights.claim: Pompliano notes the S&P's profit margin is up 58% since 2011, arguing these are structurally better digital-IP businesses that deserve higher valuations and shouldn't be compared to dot-com-era multiples.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A structural-margin argument against the 'stocks are historically overvalued' bear case.

insights.claim: Pompliano runs a barbell: large-cap indices (NASDAQ ~18%/yr over the decade) OR very asymmetric small bets, deliberately avoiding the mushy middle of mid-size companies with mid-size growth and returns.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A reusable allocation frame — durability (large cap) plus optionality (small), nothing in between.
insights.quote: I like to operate in the barbells. I don't like to operate in the middle

insights.claim: Pompliano argues SpaceX is fundamentally an AI/compute company, not a space company — its IPO deck put ~$28T of enterprise AI in a ~$30T TAM, and it has signed three compute contracts of >$100M/month in three weeks plus a Cursor acquisition and xAI ties.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Reframes SpaceX's valuation as an AI-compute story — the launch/Starlink monopoly is the base, AI is the multiple.
insights.quote: SpaceX is an AI company. That is why they are valued the way that they are valued

insights.claim: Pompliano owns Tesla as a 'picks and shovels' AI play — the value is the computer vision/ML/models/data, not the hardware — and expects Elon Musk to merge SpaceX and Tesla into a physical-AI conglomerate before 2030.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A differentiated Tesla thesis (own the models, not the cars) plus a specific SpaceX-Tesla merger catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: I actually think of Tesla as a picks and shovels company

insights.claim: Pompliano's physical-AI/robotics basket includes Tesla, Robo Strategy (a closed-end fund for private robotics), and Anduril (defense drones) — whose roll-up model is to let startups make breakthroughs, then acquire and commercialize them via a strong BD/contracts engine, now extending to land and water drones; he cites Masayoshi Son calling robotics the next trillion-dollar opportunity.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A concrete defense-AI/robotics roll-up thesis and named vehicles for public+private physical-AI exposure.
insights.quote: they acquire technology and then they go and they commercialize the technology

insights.claim: Pompliano's private software holdings (Replit, Lovable, Micro One) give him software/app-layer exposure while his public book skews physical AI/robotics — a deliberate full-stack AI position across software/hardware and public/private.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Names specific private AI-software bets and illustrates a full-stack barbell across public and private markets.

insights.claim: Pompliano's base case is Bitcoin compounding 25-30% per year for the next decade — not 10-20x bull markets — as institutional adoption compresses volatility (from ~80 vol toward 35-40), which paradoxically attracts large pools of capital seeking a durable 25-30% asset.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Sets a grounded Bitcoin return expectation; the vol compression is the feature institutions buy, not a bug.
insights.quote: My base case is 25 to 30% for the next decade

insights.claim: Pompliano says retail/internet Bitcoin sentiment is terrible (capitulation, rage-quitting) while institutional sentiment is calm and constructive (Franklin Templeton, OKX-ICE partnerships, custody build-out), and that the information split has shifted from 100/0 to ~50/50 internet-vs-institutions.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Internet sentiment is no longer a reliable Bitcoin proxy — the institutional layer is now half the signal and points to possible bottoming.

insights.claim: Pompliano's core philosophy: he WANTS assets that go in and out of favor, because out-of-favor means more asymmetry, whereas always-popular assets are crowded and their returns arbed away.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: A contrarian mandate — embrace thematic volatility and unpopularity as the source of forward return.
insights.quote: I do not want to own assets that are always in favor
